1: Paul's departure from Ephesus follows the riot of Acts 19:23–41. The Macedonian journey mirrors the second missionary journey's Macedonian phase (Acts 16:10–Acts 17:15) — revisiting communities Paul had planted. The 'exhorting' (parakalesas) of the disciples before departure reflects Paul's consistent pattern of pastoral consolidation before transitions, preparing communities for his absence.
2: The 'those parts' (ta merē ekeina) traversed in Macedonia likely included Philippi, Thessalonica, and Berea. 'Much exhortation' (paraklēsei pollē) summarizes the pastoral content without detail — Luke's interest is the geographical itinerary, not the content of the visits. The three months in Greece (Acts 20:3) would include Corinth, which Paul had established on the second journey and revisited on the third (cf. 2 Corinthians 12:14; Acts 13:1).
3: The Jewish plot against Paul at Corinth echoes the pattern of opposition Paul faces at the end of each major phase of ministry (cf. Acts 9:23; Acts 14:19; Acts 17:5; Acts 19:9). The decision to return through Macedonia rather than sail directly from Corinth to Syria reflects genuine security concerns — a ship passage through the Aegean offered ambush opportunities at embarkation points. The rerouting adds weeks to the journey but removes the threat.
4: The list of companions accompanying Paul to Jerusalem with the collection (cf. 1 Corinthians 16:3–4; 2 Cor 8–9; Romans 15:25–28) represents the geographical breadth of the Gentile mission: Sopater of Berea (Macedonia), Aristarchus and Secundus of Thessalonica (Macedonia), Gaius of Derbe and Timothy (Galatia/South Asia Minor), and Tychicus and Trophimus of Asia. These men likely served as official delegates from their communities, carrying the collection funds and giving it legal standing as a communal gift rather than a personal transfer.
5: The 'we' narrative resumes here after the gap since Acts 16:17 — Luke rejoins the party at Philippi (cf. Acts 16:40, where the 'we' dropped out after Paul's departure). The resumption 'these men went ahead and waited for us at Troas' indicates Luke traveled separately from the main group, meeting at the familiar Troas transit point (cf. Acts 16:8–11). The five days from Philippi to Troas (contrast the two-day favorable crossing of Acts 16:11) suggests adverse winds.
6: The 'days of Unleavened Bread' (Azymōn) — the week of Passover — situates this journey in the spring calendar, consistent with Paul's goal of reaching Jerusalem by Pentecost (Acts 20:16). Passover to Pentecost is fifty days; the seven-day stay at Troas plus subsequent travel to Jerusalem fits the timeline. The voyage from Philippi/Neapolis to Troas taking five days (vs. two days on the second journey, Acts 16:11) suggests headwinds on the Aegean — a common spring sailing challenge.
7: The gathering 'on the first day of the week' (tē mia tōn sabbatōn) is an early attestation of Christian Sunday worship — the day of resurrection rather than the Jewish Sabbath. 'Breaking bread' (klasai arton) was the standard early Christian term for the Eucharistic meal (cf. Acts 2:42, 46; 1 Corinthians 10:16; Acts 11:20–34). The evening setting suggests the meeting followed the Jewish day reckoning (evening to evening) or simply that the community met after the work day. The extended discourse until midnight (mechri mesonyktiou) reflects Paul's awareness that this would be his last visit — he speaks at unusual length.
8: The 'many lamps' (lampades hikanai) in the upper room (hyperōō) serve both practical and narrative functions — they explain the drowsy atmosphere (heat, late hour, packed room) and establish the setting for Eutychus's fall. The upper room (hyperōon) was the standard meeting space for the early Jerusalem community (Acts 1:13) and here appears in Troas. The domestic setting — a third-floor apartment in a multistory building — is consistent with urban Hellenistic housing in a port city.
9: Eutychus ('Lucky' — a common slave name meaning fortunate) sat in the window (thyridi) and fell from the third floor (apo tou tristegou). Ancient multistory buildings in port cities like Troas could be three or four stories — the fall from the third story (tristegon) would typically be fatal. The young man (neanias) is described as 'overcome by sleep' (katapheromenos hypnō bathei) during Paul's discourse — a detail that humanizes the narrative while excusing neither Paul's length nor the congregation's drowsiness.
10: Paul's gesture — 'falling on him and embracing him' (epipesōn ... synperielaben) — deliberately echoes Elijah's action over the widow's son (1 Kings 17:21) and Elisha's over the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:34). The parallel marks Paul as a prophet in the succession of Israel's great miracle-workers. 'His life is in him' (hē psychē autou en autō estin) is the announcement of restoration — the community's response ('they were not a little comforted,' Acts 20:12) confirms the miracle.
11: After the miracle, the breaking of bread (klasas ton arton) and extended conversation (homilēsas epi poly — 'talked extensively') until dawn complete the vigil. The Eucharistic meal appears to follow the miracle rather than precede it — the celebration of resurrection life in the assembly is enacted immediately after a literal restoration of life. Paul departs at dawn, having kept the community through a full night of worship, word, and miracle.
12: The community takes Eutychus home alive (zōnta) — Luke's emphasis. The word 'comforted' (pareклēthēsan) — paraklēthēsan — echoes Paul's own mission vocabulary (parakaleō, Acts 20:1) and the title of the Spirit in John's Gospel. The Troas community that began with Paul's vision of a Macedonian man calling him (Acts 16:9) is now consolidated with this dramatic pastoral encounter — a church that will have this story as a founding narrative.
13: The coastal journey from Troas to Assos on foot (about 30 km) while the ship went around the cape was deliberate — Paul 'had so arranged' (diatetagmenos) this for himself. The reason is not given; possibly Paul wanted time alone before the intense Jerusalem journey, possibly pastoral business in the interior. Assos was a port city in the Troad with a significant harbor — the ship picked Paul up there for the coastal voyage south.
14: The coastal itinerary from Assos through Mitylene (Lesbos), Chios, Samos, and Miletus follows the standard Aegean coastal shipping lanes — ancient vessels hugged the coast rather than crossing open water where possible. The geography is precise: Mitylene was the main city of Lesbos, Chios was the island opposite the Asian coast, Samos was the island closest to Miletus/Ephesus. Luke's navigational detail in these verses reflects the 'we' eyewitness source.
15: The stops at Chios and Samos mark the approach to the Ionian coast. The 'next day we arrived at Miletus' — bypassing Ephesus (Acts 20:16) — placed Paul at the major port city of the southern Ionian coast. Miletus had been one of the great cities of archaic Greece and remained a significant commercial port in Roman Asia Minor, with a large harbor and substantial civic infrastructure. Its distance from Ephesus (about 50 km) was close enough for the elders to reach in a day or two.
16: Paul's decision to bypass Ephesus — where he had spent three years (Acts 20:31) — is explained by the Pentecost deadline. The fifty days between Passover and Pentecost created a fixed travel window; a stop at Ephesus would have been extended by the inevitable pastoral demands of the community. The Miletus stopover achieved the same purpose — meeting the Ephesian leadership — without the city's entanglements. Pentecost was a pilgrimage festival; Paul's urgency to be in Jerusalem for it reflects both personal devotion and the symbolic significance of the Spirit's arrival at the first Pentecost (Acts 2).
17: The summons to the 'elders' (presbyterous) of the church at Ephesus is the only speech in Acts addressed exclusively to Christian leaders rather than to Jewish or pagan audiences. The elders (presbyteroi) of Ephesus appear to be synonymous with 'overseers' (episkopous) in Acts 20:28 — the two terms were interchangeable in early church usage (cf. Philippians 1:1; Titus 1:5–7). The Miletus address is Paul's pastoral testament, delivered in anticipation of his death (Acts 20:22–25).
18: 'You yourselves know' (hymeis epistasthe) — Paul's self-defense mode, appealing to the elders' direct knowledge as his testimonial. His description of his Ephesian ministry emphasizes consistency ('from the first day I set foot in Asia') and character ('serving the Lord with all humility'). The Ephesian period (approximately AD 52–55) was Paul's longest sustained ministry in one city, the anchor of his Asian mission, and the base from which the surrounding region heard the word (Acts 19:10).
19: Paul's description of 'serving with tears and through trials from the plots of the Jews' (meta dakryōn kai peirasmōn ... epibolōn tōn Ioudaiōn) corresponds to the Ephesian narrative (Acts 19:9, 33; cf. 2 Corinthians 11:24–28; 1 Corinthians 15:32 — fighting 'wild beasts at Ephesus'). The emotional register ('tears') is unusual for Luke's Paul — the Miletus address repeatedly mentions tears (Acts 20:19, 31, 37) as the marker of genuine pastoral investment rather than mercenary service.
20: 'I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable' (ouk hypesteilamēn tōn symphrontōn) — the language of not suppressing difficult truths is the mark of a faithful messenger (cf. Ezekiel 33:7–9). The combination of 'public' (dēmosiai) and 'house to house' (kat' oikous) teaching reflects the dual-venue model of Acts: synagogue/public space plus domestic gatherings. The Ephesian church's house-church structure (cf. 1 Corinthians 16:19 — Aquila and Prisca's church at Ephesus) gave the mission its residential texture.
21: 'Testifying both to Jews and to Greeks of repentance toward God and of faith in our Lord Jesus Christ' is the core Pauline kerygmatic formula — applicable to both Jewish and Gentile audiences, requiring the same response. Metanoia (repentance) directed 'toward God' is the Jewish covenant category; pistis (faith) 'in our Lord Jesus Christ' is the specifically Christian addition. The bipartite formula — toward God, through Christ — captures the theological continuity (Jewish repentance tradition) and discontinuity (the new agent of reconciliation).
22: 'Bound in the Spirit' (dedemenos egō tō pneumati) uses the language of compulsion — Paul is under a binding that overrides his own preferences. The Spirit is the agent of the Jerusalem journey (cf. Acts 19:21 — Paul 'resolved in the Spirit'). The ignorance of what awaits him ('not knowing what will happen to me there') is then immediately modified by the prophetic warnings of Acts 20:23 — the Spirit reveals the suffering but not the specific details, enough to test Paul's resolve but not to determine his itinerary.
23: 'The Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city that imprisonment and afflictions await me' — the prophetic warnings in every city parallel the prophetic word through Agabus in Acts 21:10–11 and the disciples at Tyre (Acts 21:4). Paul's awareness of impending suffering is the context for his statement in Acts 20:24 — he is not ignorant of the cost, but counts it worth paying. This fulfills Jesus's prediction at Paul's call: 'I will show him how much he must suffer for the sake of my name' (Acts 9:16).
24: 'I do not account my life of any value to myself' (all' oudenos logou poioumai tēn psychēn timian emautō) is Paul's explicit statement of martyrdom readiness — the psychē (life/soul) held loosely in comparison to the mission. 'The ministry I received from the Lord Jesus to testify to the gospel of the grace of God' frames the mission as received rather than chosen — a trust (diakonia) entrusted by the Lord that Paul is obligated to complete. The language echoes his self-description in Galatians 1:1 and Romans 15:15–16.
25: 'None of you among whom I have gone about proclaiming the kingdom will see my face again' — Paul's farewell prophecy. The 'kingdom of God' (tēn basileian) is the Lukan summary of Paul's proclamation (cf. Acts 28:31), a comprehensive term for the eschatological reign Christ inaugurated. Whether this prophecy was fulfilled literally has been debated — Acts does not record a return to Ephesus, though some scholars argue the Pastoral Epistles presuppose a later Ephesian visit. Within Luke's narrative, this is a formal farewell.
26: 'I am innocent of the blood of all of you' (katharos eimi apo tou haimatos pantōn hymōn) echoes Ezekiel 3:17–21 and Acts 33:1–9 — the watchman who warns the city is released from guilt when the people ignore the warning. The idiom of blood-guilt goes back to Israel's legal tradition (Deuteronomy 19:10; 2 Samuel 1:16). Paul's self-justification is not self-congratulation but a legal declaration of cleared responsibility — he has been faithful to the warning commission.
27: 'The whole counsel of God' (pasan tēn boulēn tou theou) — the boulē of God is the divine plan comprehensively — not a selective presentation tailored to audience comfort but the full scope of God's purposes, including judgment, repentance, and the scandal of the cross. The elder who 'holds firm to the trustworthy word as taught' (Titus 1:9) is the embodiment of this standard; Paul models the courage required to proclaim the whole counsel without suppression.
28: 'Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers' (episkopous) — the Spirit's appointment of church leadership, not merely human appointment, is the ground of pastoral authority. The 'flock' metaphor (poimnion) is consistent with Jewish (Psalms 100:3; Jeremiah 23:1–4) and early Christian (John 10; 1 Peter 5:2–4) shepherd imagery. 'The church of God which he obtained with his own blood' — the possessive clause grounds pastoral care in the price paid for the flock: the blood of Christ.
29: 'Fierce wolves' (lykoi bareis) who 'come in among you not sparing the flock' — the wolf metaphor for false teachers enters the tradition at Matthew 7:15 and 10:16 and recurs in Christian pastoral literature. Paul's prediction anticipates the Ephesian situation addressed in the Pastoral Epistles (1 Timothy 1:3–7; 2 Timothy 1:15; Acts 4:3–4; Revelation 2:1–7) — false teachers who arose from within the Ephesian community within a generation of Paul's departure, fulfilling the dual prophecy of Acts 20:29–30.
30: 'From among your own selves men will arise speaking twisted things to draw away the disciples after them' — the internal origin of the false teachers is the more dangerous threat. External wolves can be identified; internal distortions arise from the established leadership itself. The Ignatian letters (c. AD 107–110) to Ephesus confirm Paul's prediction: Ignatius writes commending the Ephesian church for rejecting heretics but warns of ongoing danger. The Ephesian church's discernment (Revelation 2:2 — 'you have tested those who call themselves apostles') confirms the pattern.
31: 'Therefore be alert' (grēgoreite) — the imperative of the watchman tradition (Ezek 33; 1 Thessalonians 5:6; 1 Peter 5:8; Revelation 3:2). Paul grounds the call to vigilance in his own three-year example: 'for three years I did not cease night or day to admonish every one with tears.' The tears recur (cf. Acts 20:19, 37) as the marker of genuine pastoral investment. The three-year period corresponds to Acts 19's Ephesian ministry — Paul's longest single-city residence in the narrative.
32: 'I commend you to God and to the word of his grace' (tō theō kai tō logō tēs charitos autou) — Paul entrusts the Ephesian elders not to a successor leader but to God and the written/proclaimed word. This word 'is able to build you up and give you an inheritance among all those who are sanctified' — the word's efficacy without Paul's physical presence. The formulation anticipates the post-apostolic period when the community would depend on preserved teaching rather than living apostolic presence.
33: 'I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing' — the standard self-defense against charges of mercenary ministry (cf. 1 Thessalonians 2:5; 2 Corinthians 12:17–18; 1 Cor 9). Itinerant teachers and philosophers in antiquity were routinely suspected of exploiting their audiences for financial gain — the Sophists' fees were a standing controversy. Paul's refusal of financial support from communities while among them (though accepting it when absent — cf. Philippians 4:15–16) was a deliberate strategy to undercut this accusation.
34: 'These hands ministered to my necessities and to those who were with me' — Paul's manual labor as self-support is attested throughout his letters (1 Corinthians 4:12; 2 Thessalonians 3:8; Philippians 4:14–18) and by the Aquila-Priscilla tent-making partnership of Acts 18:3. The display of his hands — or the gesture toward them — is a physical demonstration accompanying the verbal claim, a rhetorically effective move before an audience who knew him personally and could verify it.
35: 'The words of the Lord Jesus how he said, It is more blessed to give than to receive' — this is the only explicit quotation of a saying of Jesus not found in the canonical Gospels. Such agrapha (unwritten sayings) were known in early Christianity; Paul's citation suggests a tradition of Jesus-sayings circulating independently of the written Gospels. The saying encapsulates the entire financial ethics of Acts 20:33–35 and grounds it in dominical authority.
36: Paul kneels and prays with the elders — kneeling prayer (theis ta gonata autou) was reserved for the most solemn supplications in Jewish and early Christian practice (cf. Daniel 6:10; Ephesians 3:14; Luke 22:41). The corporate prayer seals the pastoral testament and anticipates the farewell lament that follows. The gesture communicates the gravity of the moment — this is not a routine pastoral visit but a final commissioning.
37: 'They all wept aloud and embraced Paul and kissed him' (klauthmos ... epipesontes ... katephiloun) — the emotional intensity of the Miletus farewell is unparalleled in Acts. The verb kataphileō (kissed repeatedly, affectionately) and the falling on Paul's neck echo Genesis 45:14–15 (Joseph's reunion with Benjamin and his brothers) and Luke 15:20 (the father's welcome of the prodigal). The communal grief marks the depth of the pastoral relationship Paul had built over three years.
38: 'Grieving especially because of the word he had spoken, that they would not see his face again' — the specific cause of grief is Paul's farewell prophecy (Acts 20:25). The departure by ship — they 'accompanied him to the ship' — marks the physical severance. Luke's attention to this farewell scene, the most emotionally detailed in Acts, signals its theological importance: the apostolic age is closing, and the community must now sustain itself on the inherited word and appointed leadership.
